**Diagrams**
![M - Ladder deck bridge](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_008_m-ladder-deck-bridge-c_1.gif)
![M - Ladder deck bridge](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_008_m-ladder-deck-bridge-c_2.gif)

- Sample model;
- SH43 Fixed_CEL
- Define user discretization points (in the bridge object) to force splitting of the deck shell to line up with the cross beam locations. This is not necessarily critical in this instance, but it does make things a bit tidier. However, because the software also splits the deck shell in line with supports, orthogonal user discretization points around the support become messy as they clash with the line of the pier.
- Manually define the cross beams which aren't generated by the bridge object (the orthogonal cross beams at the abutments - this is easiest done using the replicate function on the closest parametrically-defined cross beams. Then trim the new cross beams as required so they don't extend beyond abutment lines.
- Draw vertical rigid links between the cross beams and deck shell - in this instance we've created 3 links per cross beam at 1/4 points along the cross beam which is a bit coarse but refining that is just a matter of replicating more links once the first is drawn. Create a group for the top node of the rigid links ('GROUP1').
- Select the dec

![L - Load pattern / Loa](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/sof_015_l-load-pattern-load-ca_2.png)

- In order to assign a load to selected objects, a thermal load or imposed displacement point load for example, you first need to have a load pattern defined for it.
- Load pattern
- A
- load pattern
- is the spatial distribution of a specific set of forces, displacements, temperatures, and other effects which act on a structure. Any combination of
- joints
- and elements may be subjected to loading and kinematic conditions. Each load pattern is assigned a design type (DEAD, WIND, QUAKE, etc.) which classifies the load and initiates the associated computational process.
- Load case
- A
- load case
- defines how
- load patterns
- are applied (statically or dynamically), how the structure responds (linearly or nonlinearly), and how analysis is performed (through
- modal analysis
- ,
- direct integration
- , etc.).
- After a load pattern is defined, the Define>Load cases dialogue is where users specify whether the load pattern is analysed as linear or nonlinear, statically or dynamically, with

- The depth of the structure is accounted for by locating the deck slab “plate” and the longitudinal girder “beams” at their respective centroids.
- Explicitly modelling the deck also means that composite behaviour
- no longer needs to be approximated using effective widths
- and modular ratios.
- For
- long term loads an effective modular ratio factor may still need
- to be accounted for in the model as well.
- For vertical loadings
- , modelling the vertical eccentricity of the supports is typically not important

- When using fiber hinges, ignore the displayed fiber state (A to B). Hinge states A, B, C, D, and E are used to define the moment-rotation curve of a coupled P-M2-M3 hinge. These parameters are not applicable to fiber P-M2-M3 hinges, therefore fiber-hinge state is always given as A to B.
- Fiber hinge: WSP Canada - Step-By-Step Pushover Analysis Using Csibridge , Page 9

- Lusas,
- Section properties;
- In LUSAS you need to go section utility build a section then import to model, revision to section is a difficult process, But in CSI Bridge it is a seamless process.
- Need excel file to calculate composite section properties
- It takes ages to build a shell and beam model for bridge in LUSAS
- Shrinkage and temperature need to be calculated in excel sheets, and updated by section revise
- Load optimizer only works on first stage
- I like
- Select point, line,
- Section cut is difficult in CSI Bridge
- Unlined model need to be reinstated occasionally
- Small change requires whole model analysis
- Not possible to load positioning
